request.question: Ronnie Dunn and Jenna Sanz-Agero were both formerly part of bands in which they each acted in what role?

title: Mama Don't Get Dressed Up for Nothing
Ronnie Dunn
Kix Brooks
If You See Him/If You See Her
Only in America (Brooks & Dunn song)
Jenna Sanz-Agero
Peace, Love, and Country Music
Ronnie Dunn (album)
Polyrically Uncorrect
Bleed Red
sentences: "Mama Don't Get Dressed Up For Nothing" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.
It was released in September 1996 as the third single from their CD "Borderline", and their eighteenth single overall.
The song received moderate success on the US Country chart, where it peaked at number 13, while it was a number 8 hit in Canada.

It was the first single the duo released that missed the US country top 10, breaking a streak of 17 straight top 10 entries; the next eight singles Brooks and Dunn released after this would also make the country top 10.
This is the fourth single to feature Kix Brooks on lead vocals instead of Ronnie Dunn.
Kix and Ronnie wrote this song, along with Don Cook.
Ronnie Gene Dunn (born June 1, 1953) is an American country music singer-songwriter and record executive.
In 2011, Dunn began working as a solo artist following the breakup of Brooks & Dunn.
He released his self-titled debut album for Arista Nashville on June 7, 2011, reaching the Top 10 with its lead-off single "Bleed Red".
In 2013, after leaving Arista Nashville in 2012, Dunn founded Little Will-E Records.
On April 8, 2014, Ronnie Dunn released his second solo album, "Peace, Love, and Country Music" through his own Little Will-E Records.

Leon Eric "Kix" Brooks III (born May 12, 1955) is an American country music artist, actor, and film producer best known for being one half of the duo Brooks & Dunn and host of radio's "American Country Countdown".
Prior to the duo's foundation, he was a singer and songwriter, charting twice on Hot Country Songs and releasing an album for Capitol Records.
Brooks and Ronnie Dunn comprised Brooks & Dunn for 20 years, with both members beginning solo careers.
Brooks's solo career after Brooks & Dunn has included the album "New to This Town".

"If You See Him/If You See Her" is a song written by Terry McBride, Jennifer Kimball and Tommy Lee James, and recorded by American country music artist Reba McEntire, along with the duo Brooks & Dunn.
It served as the title track to each artist's respective 1998 albums ("If You See Him" for Reba, and "If You See Her" for Brooks & Dunn), both released on June 2 of that year.
The song was concurrently promoted and distributed by both artists' labels: MCA Nashville and Arista Nashville, then the respective labels for McEntire and Brooks & Dunn.
It is the only single to feature both Kix Brooks & Ronnie Dunn on vocals.
"Only in America" is a song recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.
It was released in June 2001 as the second single from the album "Steers & Stripes".
Kix Brooks, one-half of the duo, co-wrote the song with Don Cook and Ronnie Rogers, although it features Ronnie Dunn on lead vocals.

"Only in America" was also the second of three consecutive Number One hits from that album, reaching its peak on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) charts for the week of October 27, 2001.
Jenna Sanz-Agero is the former lead singer of the female rock group Vixen, and a former president of Media 8.
She lives and works in Los Angeles, California.
Peace, Love, and Country Music is the second solo studio album by country music artist Ronnie Dunn.
The album was released on April 8, 2014 via Dunn's own record label Little Will-E Records.
"Peace, Love, and Country Music" is Dunn's first solo release since 2011's "Ronnie Dunn" released on Arista Nashville.
Ronnie Dunn is the debut solo studio album from country music artist Ronnie Dunn.
The album was released on June 7, 2011, via Arista Nashville.
The album is Dunn's first release of music in nearly 25 years; he released three singles in the 1980s without issuing an album.
Polyrically Uncorrect is the tenth studio album, and fourteenth album release overall, by American country music parodist Cledus T. Judd.
It was released on June 30, 2009 via E1 Music.
It includes the singles "Waitin' on Obama", "Garth Must Be Busy" and "(If I Had) Kellie Pickler's Boobs".
The album includes guest vocals from Ashton Shepherd, Ronnie Dunn, Jamey Johnson, Terry Eldredge (of The Grascals), Colt Ford and Daryle Singletary.
Chris Neal of "Country Weekly" gave the album three stars out of five, citing the Ronnie Dunn collaboration as a standout track.
"Bleed Red" is a song written by Tommy Lee James and Andrew Dorff, and recorded by American country music singer Ronnie Dunn.
The song is Dunn's first solo release after Brooks & Dunn disbanded in 2010.
"Bleed Red" was released to country radio on January 31, 2011.
It is intended to be the lead-off single from Dunn's first solo album, "Ronnie Dunn".

He had previously released three singles: "It's Written All Over Your Face", "She Put the Sad in All His Songs", and "Jessie"; with the first two singles charting to number 59 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Ronnie Dunn and Jenna Sanz-Agero were both formerly part of bands in which they each acted in what role?
hop1_docs: Jenna Sanz-Agero | Jenna Sanz-Agero is the former lead singer of the female rock group Vixen, and a former president of Media 8.  She lives and works in Los Angeles, California.

Rita Sanz-Agero | Rita Sanz-Agero (born November 8, 1991 in Guatemala City) is a female modern pentathlete from Guatemala.  At age sixteen, Sanz-Agero became the youngest modern pentathlete to compete at the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing, where she finished thirty-fourth in the women's event, with a score of 4,844 points.
María Isabel Sanz-Agero | María Isabel Sanz-Agero (born November 20, 1965) is a Guatemalan modern pentathlete.  She placed 31st in the women's individual event at the 2004 Summer Olympics.

Brooks & Dunn | Brooks & Dunn is an American country music duo consisting of Kix Brooks and Ronnie Dunn, both vocalists and songwriters.  The duo was founded in 1990 through the suggestion of Tim DuBois.  Before the foundation, both members were solo recording artists.  Brooks wrote number one singles for John Conlee, Nitty Gritty Dirt Band, and Highway 101; both he and Dunn also charted two solo singles a piece in the 1980s, with Brooks also releasing an album for Capitol Records in 1989.  However, they began hitting the charts together in 1991.

If You See Him/If You See Her | "If You See Him/If You See Her" is a song written by Terry McBride, Jennifer Kimball and Tommy Lee James, and recorded by American country music artist Reba McEntire, along with the duo Brooks & Dunn.  It served as the title track to each artist's respective 1998 albums ("If You See Him" for Reba, and "If You See Her" for Brooks & Dunn), both released on June 2 of that year.  The song was concurrently promoted and distributed by both artists' labels: MCA Nashville and Arista Nashville, then the respective labels for McEntire and Brooks & Dunn.  It is the only single to feature both Kix Brooks & Ronnie Dunn on vocals.

That's What She Gets for Loving Me | "That's What She Gets for Loving Me" is a song written by Ronnie Dunn and Terry McBride, and recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.  It was released in February 2004 as the third and final single from their album "Red Dirt Road".  It peaked at number 6 in the United States.

Ashley Monroe | Ashley Lauren Monroe is an American country music singer-songwriter.  She has released two solo singles on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart.  The singles "Satisfied" and "I Don't Want To" (which featured Brooks & Dunn singer Ronnie Dunn) reached No. 43 and No. 37, respectively.  Both singles were intended to be released on Monroe's debut album, "Satisfied", in 2007, but the album went unreleased.  Monroe left Columbia Records' roster in late 2007, and "Satisfied" was finally released on May 19, 2009.  In June 2011, Monroe, Miranda Lambert and Angaleena Presley formed a band called Pistol Annies.  Monroe's second studio album, "Like a Rose", was released on March 5, 2013 followed by her third album "The Blade", released on July 24, 2015.
hop1_titles: If You See Him/If You See Her
That's What She Gets for Loving Me
response.summary_1: Jenna Sanz-Agero is described as the "former lead singer of the female rock group Vixen" (passage [1]). Ronnie Dunn is described as part of Brooks & Dunn, with the duo consisting of Kix Brooks and Ronnie Dunn, "both vocalists and songwriters" (passage [4]). Bridge: both acted in singing roles (lead singer / vocalist). Next hop: retrieve a source confirming Ronnie Dunn's specific vocal title in Brooks & Dunn (e.g., "lead singer" vs "vocalist") using the quoted phrases above as anchors.
response.hop2_query: Ronnie Dunn Brooks & Dunn lead singer
hop2_docs: Ronnie Dunn | Ronnie Gene Dunn (born June 1, 1953) is an American country music singer-songwriter and record executive.  In 2011, Dunn began working as a solo artist following the breakup of Brooks & Dunn.  He released his self-titled debut album for Arista Nashville on June 7, 2011, reaching the Top 10 with its lead-off single "Bleed Red".  In 2013, after leaving Arista Nashville in 2012, Dunn founded Little Will-E Records.  On April 8, 2014, Ronnie Dunn released his second solo album, "Peace, Love, and Country Music" through his own Little Will-E Records.

Indian Summer (Brooks & Dunn song) | "Indian Summer" is a song by the American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.  It was written by the duo's members, Kix Brooks and Ronnie Dunn, along with Bob DiPiero.  As Brooks & Dunn's 49th single, it was released in May 2009 and was the lead-off single to the duo's third greatest hits album "#1s… and Then Some", released on September 8, 2009 via Arista Nashville.

Bleed Red | "Bleed Red" is a song written by Tommy Lee James and Andrew Dorff, and recorded by American country music singer Ronnie Dunn.  The song is Dunn's first solo release after Brooks & Dunn disbanded in 2010.  "Bleed Red" was released to country radio on January 31, 2011.  It is intended to be the lead-off single from Dunn's first solo album, "Ronnie Dunn".  He had previously released three singles: "It's Written All Over Your Face", "She Put the Sad in All His Songs", and "Jessie"; with the first two singles charting to number 59 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.

Kix Brooks | Leon Eric "Kix" Brooks III (born May 12, 1955) is an American country music artist, actor, and film producer best known for being one half of the duo Brooks & Dunn and host of radio's "American Country Countdown".  Prior to the duo's foundation, he was a singer and songwriter, charting twice on Hot Country Songs and releasing an album for Capitol Records.  Brooks and Ronnie Dunn comprised Brooks & Dunn for 20 years, with both members beginning solo careers.  Brooks's solo career after Brooks & Dunn has included the album "New to This Town".

Only in America (Brooks & Dunn song) | "Only in America" is a song recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.  It was released in June 2001 as the second single from the album "Steers & Stripes".  Kix Brooks, one-half of the duo, co-wrote the song with Don Cook and Ronnie Rogers, although it features Ronnie Dunn on lead vocals.  "Only in America" was also the second of three consecutive Number One hits from that album, reaching its peak on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) charts for the week of October 27, 2001.

Why Would I Say Goodbye | "Why Would I Say Goodbye" is a song written by Kix Brooks and Chris Waters, and recorded by American country music duo Brooks & Dunn.  It was released in March 1997 as the fifth and final single from their CD "Borderline".  The song reached a peak of number 8 on the US Country chart, and number 9 on the Canadian "RPM" Country Tracks chart.  This is the fifth of the six Brooks & Dunn singles in which Kix Brooks sings the lead vocals instead of Ronnie Dunn.

Sacred Ground (song) | "Sacred Ground" is a country music song, co-written and originally recorded by American singer Kix Brooks, prior to his joining Ronnie Dunn in the duo Brooks & Dunn.  Brooks' version was issued in 1989 as a single, and was included on his 1989 self-titled debut album.
response.summary_2: Both were singers: Jenna Sanz-Agero was the lead singer of Vixen, and Ronnie Dunn is a singer who frequently performed lead vocals for Brooks & Dunn (e.g., "Only in America" features Ronnie Dunn on lead vocals).
